steps.title: Match exposure to distinction
steps.content: The team's conclusion is about previous language exposure affecting how well dogs distinguish familiar languages from unfamiliar ones. Strong support must connect greater exposure with a stronger difference between the brain response to a familiar language and the brain response to an unfamiliar language.

steps.title: Separate language from noise
steps.content: Choice B compares scrambled non-speech with an unfamiliar language, and Choice D compares a familiar language with scrambled non-speech. Those findings may involve speech detection, but they do not directly test the familiar-language versus unfamiliar-language distinction.

steps.title: Reject the flat-age result
steps.content: Choice C says dogs of all ages showed the same kind of familiar-language advantage, so it does not show exposure level matters. Older dogs have generally had more exposure, and Choice A says their language-response gap was greatest. The answer is A.
